an3.63:1.1: At one time, the *Bhagavā* was wandering on tour among the Kosalans together with a large *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus*, and arrived at Venāgapura, a brahmin village of the Kosalans.
an3.63:1.2: The brahmin householders of Venāgapura heard:
an3.63:1.3: "The ascetic Gotama — a son of the Sakyans who went forth from the Sakyan clan — has arrived at Venāgapura.
an3.63:1.4: That good Gotama has this fine reputation that has spread:
an3.63:1.5: 'He is the *Bhagavā*, an *arahant*, fully enlightened, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, well-gone, knower of the world, unsurpassed trainer of persons to be tamed, teacher of gods and humans, a Buddha, a *Bhagavā*.'
an3.63:1.6: He has, by his own direct knowledge, realized and proclaimed this world with its devas, Māra, and Brahmā, with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans.
an3.63:1.7: He teaches *Dhamma* that is good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good at the end, with the right meaning and phrasing; he reveals the holy life that is wholly complete and pure.
an3.63:1.8: Good indeed is the seeing of such *arahants*."
an3.63:10.1: But what, Master Gotama, is the noble high and luxurious bed of which Master Gotama now obtains as he wishes, without difficulty, without trouble?"
an3.63:10.10: If I, brahmin, being in such a state, stand …
an3.63:10.12: lie down, then at that time my high and luxurious bed is noble.
an3.63:10.13: This, brahmin, is the noble high and luxurious bed of which I now obtain as I wish, without difficulty, without trouble."
an3.63:10.2: "Here, brahmin, whatever village or town I am dwelling near, in the morning I dress, take my bowl and robe, and enter that very village or town for alms.
an3.63:10.3: Having returned from the alms round, after the meal, I enter the forest.
an3.63:10.4: Whatever grass or leaves are there, I gather them together, sit down cross-legged, set my body upright, and establish *sati* in front of me.
an3.63:10.6: 'Lust has been abandoned by me, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made to be no more, incapable of arising in the future;
an3.63:10.7: hatred has been abandoned by me, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made to be no more, incapable of arising in the future;
an3.63:10.8: delusion has been abandoned by me, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made to be no more, incapable of arising in the future.'
an3.63:10.9: If I, brahmin, being in such a state, walk, then at that time my walk is noble.
an3.63:11.2: Who else could obtain such a noble high and luxurious bed as he wishes, without difficulty, without trouble, other than Master Gotama?
an3.63:12.1: Magnificent, Master Gotama! Magnificent, Master Gotama!
an3.63:12.2: Just as one might right what was overturned, reveal what was hidden, show the path to one who is lost, or hold an oil lamp in the darkness so that those with eyes can see forms — in the same way, Master Gotama has made the *Dhamma* clear in many ways.
an3.63:12.3: We go to Master Gotama for refuge, and to the *Dhamma*, and to the *Bhikkhusaṅgha*.
an3.63:12.4: Let Master Gotama remember us as lay followers who have gone for refuge from this day forth for life."
an3.63:2.1: Then the brahmin householders of Venāgapura went to the *Bhagavā*. Having approached, some paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat to one side; some exchanged pleasantries with the *Bhagavā*, and having exchanged greetings and pleasing conversation, sat to one side; some raised their hands in salutation toward the *Bhagavā* and sat to one side; some announced their name and clan to the *Bhagavā* and sat to one side; some sat to one side in silence. Seated to one side, Vacchagotta the brahmin of Venāgapura said this to the *Bhagavā*:
an3.63:3.10: a couch, a sofa, a woolen coverlet, an embroidered coverlet, a white woolly coverlet, a long woolly coverlet, a cotton quilt, a fringed woolen blanket, a fur rug with hair on both sides, a fur rug with hair on one side, a silk-and-gem cloth, a silk coverlet, a carpet, an elephant cloth, a horse cloth, a chariot cloth, an antelope-hide rug, a superior deer-hide rug, a couch with a canopy above and red cushions at both ends — surely Master Gotama obtains such high and luxurious beds as he wishes, without difficulty, without trouble?"
an3.63:3.2: How serene are Master Gotama's *indriyas*, how pure and bright is his complexion.
an3.63:3.3: Just as, Master Gotama, a jujube fruit ripened in autumn is pure and bright,
an3.63:3.4: so too are Master Gotama's *indriyas* serene, his complexion pure and bright.
an3.63:3.5: Just as, Master Gotama, a palm fruit freshly released from its stem is pure and bright,
an3.63:3.6: so too are Master Gotama's *indriyas* serene, his complexion pure and bright.
an3.63:3.7: Just as, Master Gotama, an ornament of Jambonada gold, well worked by a skilled smith's son, placed on a yellow blanket, shines, glows, and radiates,
an3.63:3.8: so too are Master Gotama's *indriyas* serene, his complexion pure and bright.
an3.63:3.9: Those high and luxurious beds — namely:
an3.63:4.2: a couch, a sofa, a woolen coverlet, an embroidered coverlet, a white woolly coverlet, a long woolly coverlet, a cotton quilt, a fringed woolen blanket, a fur rug with hair on both sides, a fur rug with hair on one side, a silk-and-gem cloth, a silk coverlet, a carpet, an elephant cloth, a horse cloth, a chariot cloth, an antelope-hide rug, a superior deer-hide rug, a couch with a canopy above and red cushions at both ends —
an3.63:4.3: those are hard to obtain for those who have gone forth, and even if obtained, they would not be allowable.
an3.63:5.1: There are, brahmin, three high and luxurious beds of which I now obtain as I wish, without difficulty, without trouble.
an3.63:5.3: The divine high and luxurious bed, the brahmic high and luxurious bed, and the noble high and luxurious bed.
an3.63:5.4: These, brahmin, are the three high and luxurious beds of which I now obtain as I wish, without difficulty, without trouble."
an3.63:6.1: "But what, Master Gotama, is the divine high and luxurious bed of which Master Gotama now obtains as he wishes, without difficulty, without trouble?"
an3.63:6.10: If I, brahmin, being in such a state, stand, then at that time my standing is divine.
an3.63:6.11: If I, brahmin, being in such a state, sit, then at that time my sitting is divine.
an3.63:6.12: If I, brahmin, being in such a state, lie down, then at that time my high and luxurious bed is divine.
an3.63:6.13: This, brahmin, is the divine high and luxurious bed of which I now obtain as I wish, without difficulty, without trouble."
an3.63:6.2: "Here, brahmin, whatever village or town I am dwelling near, in the morning I dress, take my bowl and robe, and enter that very village or town for alms.
an3.63:6.3: Having returned from the alms round, after the meal, I enter the forest.
an3.63:6.4: Whatever grass or leaves are there, I gather them together, sit down cross-legged, set my body upright, and establish *sati* in front of me.
an3.63:6.5: Secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unwholesome *dhammas*, I enter and dwell in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*].
an3.63:6.6: With the stilling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with internal confidence and unification of mind [*cetaso ekodibhāvaṁ*], I enter and dwell in the second *jhāna*, which is without thought [*vitakka*] and without examination [*vicāra*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi*.
an3.63:6.7: With the fading of *pīti*, dwelling with equanimity [*upekkhā*], with *sati* and fully aware [*sampajānā*], experiencing *sukha* with the body, I enter and dwell in the third *jhāna*, of which the noble ones say: 'One with equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati* dwells in *sukha*.'
an3.63:6.8: With the abandoning of *sukha* and *dukkha*, and with the earlier passing away of joy and grief, I enter and dwell in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither *dukkha* nor *sukha*, with the purity of equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati*.
an3.63:6.9: If I, brahmin, being in such a state, walk, then at that time my walk is divine.
an3.63:7.2: Who else could obtain such a divine high and luxurious bed as he wishes, without difficulty, without trouble, other than Master Gotama?
an3.63:8.1: But what, Master Gotama, is the brahmic high and luxurious bed of which Master Gotama now obtains as he wishes, without difficulty, without trouble?"
an3.63:8.10: If I, brahmin, being in such a state, stand …
an3.63:8.12: lie down, then at that time my high and luxurious bed is brahmic.
an3.63:8.13: This, brahmin, is the brahmic high and luxurious bed of which I now obtain as I wish, without difficulty, without trouble."
an3.63:8.2: "Here, brahmin, whatever village or town I am dwelling near, in the morning I dress, take my bowl and robe, and enter that very village or town for alms.
an3.63:8.3: Having returned from the alms round, after the meal, I enter the forest.
an3.63:8.4: Whatever grass or leaves are there, I gather them together, sit down cross-legged, set my body upright, and establish *sati* in front of me.
an3.63:8.5: I dwell pervading one direction with a mind [*citta*] accompanied by *mettā*, likewise the second, likewise the third, likewise the fourth; and so above, below, and across, everywhere, to all as to myself, I dwell pervading the entire world with a mind [*citta*] accompanied by *mettā* — vast, exalted, immeasurable, without enmity, without ill will.
an3.63:8.8: I dwell pervading one direction with a mind [*citta*] accompanied by *upekkhā*, likewise the second, likewise the third, likewise the fourth; and so above, below, and across, everywhere, to all as to myself, I dwell pervading the entire world with a mind [*citta*] accompanied by *upekkhā* — vast, exalted, immeasurable, without enmity, without ill will.
an3.63:8.9: If I, brahmin, being in such a state, walk, then at that time my walk is brahmic.
an3.63:9.2: Who else could obtain such a brahmic high and luxurious bed as he wishes, without difficulty, without trouble, other than Master Gotama?
